body:
> Maybe we should first check if there are any unsaved scenes (etc.). If there are, we could skip showing `Stop & Quit`. 

I don't have a strong preference and the behaviour shown in those videos is acceptable to me.

That said, I do think there is minor value in doing the action at the time it is requested before moving to the next step. For example, if the user taps "stop & quit", I expect the game to be immediately stopped and then the quit should happen, which would prompt to save the scene. If the order was backwards, I would similarly expect that when the "save" button is tapped, it would save immediately and then prompt if you want to stop. Does that make sense? (Sorry, that was a bit awkward to write.)

body:
Hello, thank you for contributing to the Godot engine!
Proposals for adding new features should be associated with a [feature proposal](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues). Most importantly, it should include a use-case for merging complex polygons together. Examples with images would be most appreciated.
author: Ivorforce

body: Hi! Thanks for the feedback! This is working towards the feature proposal linked, though I took a bit of a different approach to solving the issue than in the feature proposal that I thought was a bit simpler. https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10395 for some examples with images, let's say I wanted to merge this polygon (stored in data as a PackedVector2Array since it is a simple contour). with this one (also a simple 2D contour stored as a PackedVector2Array). I can use the builtin Geometry2D function merge_polygons! However, in this case (and in many others) this merge operation will return an array of 2D polygons comprising both the outer contour and a hole generated while merging the two (the outer contour is shown in blue and the hole is shown in yellow below). However, all the current Geometry2D merge operations only take in individual PackedVector2Arrays as inputs, so merging two polygons with holes (represented by arrays of PackedVector2Arrays) quickly can become quite a complicated scripting task. The clipper2d library has the functionality built-in to merge these arrays of polygons efficiently, so this PR is simply to expose those functions to GDScript so people can use them! Let me know if this feature proposal + explanation is what you are looking for, happy to make a new feature proposal or expand further on this if that is necessary, thanks!

author: Calinou

body: The linked proposal is more complex for good reason. It tries to abstract away all the performance obstacles that scripting has by keeping things internal until the final commit. A boolean ops API that needs to shuffle and return entire TypedArray<Vector> returns all the time to users in scripting does not performance scale at all.
author: smix8

body:
Ahh yep, makes a lot of sense on the performance side of things. My thinking behind this approach unlock people to have this capability in a small way, and then if large-scale merging is a thing many people want to do and the performance is needed, it doesn't close the door to what was in the proposal later. Is that kinda philosophical approach preferred for godot, or would we rather go for something bigger from the start? 

Another question I have is does the CSG PR linked here kinda make these changes to Geometry2D just not needed anymore or do you see both things needing to be done? 

Thanks!
author: samjudd

body:
CSG2D and the linked GeometryOperations from the proposal try to solve the boolean ops need for two major but very different Godot user groups.

The CSG2D is targeted at beginners and "code challenged" users that like to use Nodes for their ease of use and visual feedback, e.g. a gameplay or level designer on a project that needs to test things out but can not necessarily write working geometry involving code. For smaller projects that is also all that people need for performance.

The GeometryOperations is for all users that can work with code as it is a scaleable solution that works to any level of complexity. It also can work with threads while nodes have a natural problem with threads. Being a reusable RefCounted it also does not trash language binds with garbage collectors like CSharp at the same level as a full array returning function does.

I am the wrong person to ask about "Does Godot need a medium-difficult-medium-performance solution?" because my personal answer is always no. I have never encountered those medium-medium users in all those years. If someone can code they learn even server api use within less than an hour given the right documentation.

author: Meorge

body:
Thanks for the approval! 😄 

I agree with the month thing. I suppose a month *could* be defined as 1/12 of a year? But in practice, I could see that resulting in issues, where the number of months calculated doesn't align with actual months, due to their varying lengths and leap days etc, as you suggested.

I could add a `DURATION_MONTHS` enum value as `1 << 5`, and shift `DURATION_YEARS` up to `1 << 6`, so that if we come up with a way to do months reliably, we're not either breaking compatibility with enum values or messing up the order of lengths.
author: Meorge

body:
I think it would be good to add a "months" enum value.
I don't think people will check the "4 months ago" dates, see that it was only 3.95 months ago due to february, and complain. I think it's expected that it's a "roughly 30 days" kind of value. The biggest risk would be people using it for very long time spans and expecting it to be accurate, but I don't think this should be a concern for this API.

As such, I think approximating it as 30.5 days would be good. This value (very slightly greater than `12/365`) should ensure we won't get a "13 months ago" kind of value if "years,months" was requested.
author: Ivorforce

body:
Hi! I'm not convinced this API is better than the alternative of having the following methods:
- `Time.get_seconds`, `Time.get_minutes`, `Time.get_hours`, `Time.get_days`, `Time.get_weeks` methods, which take a duration and return a wrapped integer of seconds/minutes/hours/etc (for example, seconds and minutes are 0-59 and hours are 0-23)
- `Time.get_total_minutes`, `Time.get_total_hours`, `Time.get_total_days`, `time.get_total_weeks` methods, which return a float of the input duration converted to those units
- `Time.from_minutes`, `Time.from_hours`, `Time.from_days`, and `Time.from_weeks`, which are the inverses of the above (take a input time in those units, convert to seconds)

The *primary usecase* for this API seems to be "I want `HH:MM:SS` time" and similar usecases. For exactly that usecase, a *time formatting API* (ala perhaps C#'s, see [standard timespan format strings](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/standard-timespan-format-strings) and [custom timespan format strings](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-timespan-format-strings)) is a better solution. For other usecases, the dictionary doesn't *add anything* to this API. Most users will be storing minutes/seconds explicitly in local variables or properties on their class, for example, and using a dictionary opens the way for various dynamic typing mistakes to be made while also being trivial to recreate yourself if needed.

The "edges" of this API, like the fact you could specify TOTAL_MINUTES and TOTAL_YEARS then get a meaningful result, are so little traversed I'm not sure they're useful to have.

This take is inspired both by how python handles this (a dedicated datetime type, with properties that compute seconds/minutes/etc as needed, and an analogous [dedicated ideal time type](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.time.hour)) and how C# handles this (a dedicated datetime type, and [a dedicated timespan type](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.timespan?view=net-10.0) that is mostly analogous to my proposal except using properties instead)

I've never seen an API like this in any of the languages I've used and I'm not convinced it is meaningfully better than more common API approaches.

Related, I similarly think Years are not useful to provide. The actual duration of a year, like a month, is variable. Having it be fractional is *surprising*, as you end up with days that are part of two different months or two different years depending on how far along you are in that day. For some of the usecases of an API like this, like in-game timing if using essentially "accelerated realtime", having the day not change in step with the year/month is odd, and the definitions of both likely don't match what the programmer wants. Neither python not C# provide any methods for getting months/years out of `TimeSpan`s/`time`, because it's a nonsensical question.

For years/months specifically i think it's best to try to lead people toward the pit of success and simply say "if you want these, you're on the hook for deciding what is right for your situation". In some situations, the right answer is to add the duration to the current wall clock time and use wall clock timing to figure out the number of months/years passed. In others, it's to arbitrarily pick because you don't actually *care* about the duration of them, you just need to time something to occur roughly once a month, etc.

Currently with this API as proposed, the equivalent for "I want seconds 0-59" is `Time.get_duration_dict_from_duration(duration, Time.DURATION_SECONDS)["seconds"]`, and the equivalent for "I want the total number of days" is `duration / 86400 # there's no helper for this`.

Those are both super common usecases, which this somewhat overkill API completely misses. And conversely if you did the API I proposed, this API is mostly moot outside of the niche edges I mentioned earlier, you can just make the dict yourself if you absolutely need it.

I encourage Godot to consider a more cohesive time API, with dedicated types, instead of the current situation.
author: moonheart08

body:
Thanks for the feedback and suggestions!

---

> I'm not convinced this API is better than the alternative of having the following methods:
> - Time.get_seconds, Time.get_minutes, Time.get_hours, Time.get_days, Time.get_weeks methods, which take a duration and return a wrapped integer of seconds/minutes/hours/etc (for example, seconds and minutes are 0-59 and hours are 0-23)
> - Time.get_total_minutes, Time.get_total_hours, Time.get_total_days, time.get_total_weeks methods, which return a float of the input duration converted to those units
> - Time.from_minutes, Time.from_hours, Time.from_days, and Time.from_weeks, which are the inverses of the above (take a input time in those units, convert to seconds)

The proposed `Time.get_whole_*` (name slightly changed to improve clarity), `Time.get_total_*`, and `Time.from_*` methods sound to me like they would be quite feasible to implement. I suppose I'd need to do some more rigorous testing to "prove" it, but I feel like this family of methods makes assumptions about the units the user wants, which may not necessarily be correct.

---

> The primary usecase for this API seems to be "I want HH:MM:SS time" and similar usecases. For exactly that usecase, a time formatting API (ala perhaps C#'s, see [standard timespan format strings](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/standard-timespan-format-strings) and [custom timespan format strings](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-timespan-format-strings)) is a better solution.

The time formatting API sounds to me like it could be a useful solution in the longer run, but would still require the underlying work of separating a number of seconds out into whatever time components the user wants for their formatted string. (See below for more discussion of fleshing out a full time API for Godot.)

---

> The "edges" of this API, like the fact you could specify TOTAL_MINUTES and TOTAL_YEARS then get a meaningful result, are so little traversed I'm not sure they're useful to have.

I can definitely understand this perspective; the number of cases where someone will want a nonstandard splitting of time like this will be very few, compared to something like HH:MM:SS. However I don't know if I would consider it to be a point *against* this design. In my opinion at least, the design is intuitive for regular users with no ambiguity about how the input time is being split up, and does allow for weird edge cases like minutes and years if a user happens to want that.

---

> Related, I similarly think Years are not useful to provide. The actual duration of a year, like a month, is variable. Having it be fractional is surprising, as you end up with days that are part of two different months or two different years depending on how far along you are in that day. ... Neither python not C# provide any methods for getting months/years out of TimeSpans/time, because it's a nonsensical question.

As a counterargument to this, [C++20's `std::chrono::duration`](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/chrono/duration.html) defines:
- 1 year = 365.2425 days
- 1 month = 30.436875 days (1/12 of 1 year)

While not _perfect_, it's probably plenty good enough for 99% of use cases. Perhaps we could include notes in the documentation for these methods that emphasize that the values, especially at these scales, are approximations, and that they should consider a more robust time library if that isn't acceptable.

---

> Currently with this API as proposed, the equivalent for "I want seconds 0-59" is
> ```gdscript
> Time.get_duration_dict_from_duration(duration, Time.DURATION_SECONDS)["seconds"]
> ```

I believe this would actually be
```gdscript
Time.get_duration_dict_from_duration(duration, Time.DURATION_MINUTES | Time.DURATION_SECONDS)["seconds"]
```
so that all of the whole minutes get sorted into the `"minutes"` value of the resulting dictionary, and only the remaining whole seconds are in the `"seconds"` value.

---

> and the equivalent for "I want the total number of days" is `duration / 86400 # there's no helper for this`.

This could be (mostly) done with:
```gdscript
Time.get_duration_dict_from_duration(duration, Time.DURATION_DAYS)["days"]
```
This should give you the number of _whole_ days; if there's a fractional part, then those would indeed be in `"remaining_seconds"` and would have to be multiplied up to a fraction of a day. To address this, we could add an optional bool argument that, when enabled, puts the remaining seconds into the fractional part of the smallest unit specified by the user.

---

> I encourage Godot to consider a more cohesive time API, with dedicated types, instead of the current situation.

In the long run I agree this would be best. However, I'm worried that the process of fully designing and implementing such an API would take a very long time, if it was ever completed. In the (long) meantime, there would be no easy/built-in solution, and users would have to perform these standard calculations themselves. In other words, I worry this could end up being an instance of "perfect is the enemy of good".

---

Overall, I'm currently thinking it would make sense to:
- Implement this PR for now, seeing as it has maintainer approval and general community support.
  - We can add notes to `DURATION_YEARS` (and `DURATION_MONTHS`, if added) stating that they are approximations and should not be relied upon to be ultra-precise. Users are encouraged to find a dedicated time-handling library if they need especially precise numbers at that scale.
  - A flag could be added that fills the smallest time component specified with the remaining seconds.
  - I think implementing your proposed `get_whole_*`, `get_total_*`, and `from_*` methods as well (but in a separate PR) could also be good.
- Open a proposal/discussion thread for a more robust time-handling API built into Godot. We can discuss how something would be built for Godot that more closely resembles things like C#'s time formatting and time span APIs.
  - My understanding is that time math can get to be very difficult due to lots of strange edge cases and inconsistencies. Because of this, we might want to have the Godot API wrap around an existing, well-maintained C++ library for handling these sorts of things.
- If/when the aforementioned API is implemented and merged into Godot and its functionality encompasses `get_duration_dict_from_duration()`, the function can be marked as deprecated.
author: Meorge

body:
Thanks for the reviews, everyone! I've incorporated the changes.

I've also added `DURATION_WEEKS` and `DURATION_MONTHS`, following [`std::chrono::duration`'s helper types according to cppreference.com](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/chrono/duration.html). One week is defined as 7 days, of course, and one month is defined as 30.436875 days, or 1/12 of a year. (As I was putting together the calculations for these values, I realized this means that a month is then technically defined as 4.348125 weeks, which is weird...? But it seems the math still lines up with what the C++20 standard is, so I'll roll with it.)

In addition, I updated the documentation for the method and enum values to emphasize that the month and year calculations are not exact, due to those units not being as well-defined as smaller ones. I expect most use cases of this method to stay with the smaller units, but just in case, I think it's good for people to be told upfront that things could get imprecise if they choose to use it for large-scale time.

body:
> It would be better to mention the installation script here, or automatically run it if `env["swappy"]` is true and swappy is missing:

I've added a mention of the script in the warning. I think in the future, we should probably move towards making Swappy a hard requirement to build Godot with the default options, so that we can ensure all APKs are built with it enabled. The installation script should remove most of the friction already. We could keep support for explicitly passing `swappy=no` for those who really need it, but I would ensure the default options lead to an APK with a good user experience.

Running scripts that download things from the Internet without prompting the user is a contentious issue in a buildsystem though (Linux distributions and Flathub's build system don't allow it). Therefore, the script should still be run manually before building for now.

body: I am testing this in more situations and it seems that dynamically spawning a new thread isn't possible right now and causes a deadlock. We access both the `threads` vector and the `thread_id` hashmap without locking. So already we have potential for a race condition, but it seems like somehow the task_mutex is being held and not released for some reason. Debugging further now
author: clayjohn

body:
Just a brief update. The code is working correctly as far as I can tell and we are never allocating two pump tasks on a single thread. This should remove one case of deadlocks. 

However, one of my test cases is the situation where we have more pump tasks than threads. In that case, I am getting a deadlock with the current code (which adds a new thread). The previous version of this PR worked "correctly", but the correct behaviour in that case was to crash with a warning to the user. 

With this version of the PR, when there are more pump tasks than threads, we correctly allocate additional threads, but then the engine deadlocks. I can't figure out exactly why it is deadlocking though. 

For those interested, here is a simple thread dump exhibiting the problem:

The main thread is stuck waiting to synchronize with the PhysicsServer3D while the PhysicsServer3D is stuck yielding to the WTP. In theory, as soon as the main thread requests a sync, the worker thread running the PhysicsServer3D should unlock and stop yielding. But for some reason that isn't happening.

```
2495 Thread_18215694   DispatchQueue_1: com.apple.main-thread  (serial)
    + 2495 start  (in dyld) + 2476  [0x187d1b154]

author: clayjohn

body:
I figured out the issue. The new code is breaking because ThreadData/thread cannot be safely relocated. When the LocalVector `threads` is resized, the pointers to the elements are moved, but we store the pointer to the ThreadData when launching each thread, so we cannot move them at run time. 

The problem goes away if I resize to a larger size up front or reserve enough.
author: clayjohn

body:
> Isn't this whole situation something that can be foreseen from a specific combination of settings? Perhaps we can just predict when too many pump tasks will be needed for too little threads, and compensate by printing an error like "Too many dedicated threads were set up in the project settings for the amount of available dedicated threads. Please adjust the settings accordingly. Compensating by spawning as many threads as necessary for the given dedicated thread settings." If properly predicted, we also wouldn't have to reserve / resize `threads` at all.

Almost. In 3 out of 4 cases we can predict the needed number of threads in advance. See my comment above https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108697#discussion_r2222483493

I don't love this solution as it requires us to basically hard code the project settings in WTP::init() . But in that regard it isn't all that different from the reserve hack since both require an idea of how many potential pump tasks there are. 


Pump tasks are command queues that are associated with a specific thread in the WTP. Basically they are systems that have been assigned a dedicated thread. The thread is yielded to the WTP when the command queues is empty. But as soon as something is added to the queue, the thread returns to processing that queue.
author: clayjohn

body:
> Thanks for explaining!
> 
> How does this whole 'pump task' thing work out in non-threaded builds? If it just runs the leftover command queues on the main thread, could we imitate that for 'leftover' command queues that didn't get a dedicated thread in the WTP?
> 
> Otherwise, while I'm still a bit sceptical about pump tasks in general, I see no reason not to merge this if it solves a release blocker. It seems like a fitting solution for the problem at hand.

I believe if you use one of the separate thread options in non threaded builds the engine will just deadlock. That's a good reminder though. All of this added code should be behind an `#ifdef THREADS_ENABLED` check

author: clayjohn

body:
> Just to confirm, this fixes the infinite loop because the size was incorrect before?

It's not, size is the same, and it's not an infinite loop.

`get_pixel` is not supported by compressed textures, so it's spamming errors for every pixel in the texture (x2, since it's two nested loops), and logging errors is not particularly fast (and getting slower as the number of messages increase). It will probably finish in a few minutes (if the texture is small enough and the editor won't run out of memory or message queue space before, which is likely what will happen).

*Edit:*
> *if the texture is small enough and the editor won't run out of memory or message queue space before, which is likely what will happen*

*tested it with MRP from the issue, as predicted, crashes with `Failed method: EditorToaster::_error_handler_impl. Message queue out of memory. Message queue out of memory. Try increasing 'memory/limits/message_queue/max_size_mb' in project settings.` after 6 min (about half way).*

With decompressed texture, `get_pixel` should always work.

> **Make sure that the PR comes in one commit.**

> When each commit is self-contained and could be used to build a clean and working version of the engine, it may be okay to merge a pull request with multiple commits, but in general, we require that all pull requests only have one commit. This helps us keep the Git history clean.

This PR is 5 commits. I have taken care that each commit is logically independent, can be built individually, and makes a distinct change, but if preferred I can squash this down into fewer commits.

body: Ultimately, I've decided to change the design of this feature to add an optional `mipmap_limit` parameter to the necessary methods instead. The reasoning behind this is that it now functions the same way as the mipmap limit setting in `ResourceImproterTexture`. Additionally, partial mipmaps are an advanced feature that shouldn't get in the way of common users, so this new approach was designed with that in mind.

body:
> I'd go as far to say as we should consider squeezing this into 4.5. As a new interface, it should be impossible for this to introduce new bugs, and it would be nice to already use it with godot-cpp in the 4.5 release (rather than in half a year from now).

The risk is more that an API is stabilized that then has to be indefinitely supported, whereas adding it after would give the whole 4.6 cycle for review, testing and feedback. At least this was the reasoning for the `RequiredPtr` change, even though one part (the `meta` information) could have been added in a purely backwards-compatible way.

But yes, lately there are only 2 Godot releases per year, which isn't great for GDExtension which is tied to minor releases. It comes down to a lot of these make-or-break scenarios :confused:
author: Bromeon

body:
@Ivorforce:

> I'd go as far to say as we should consider squeezing this into 4.5. As a new interface, it should be impossible for this to introduce new bugs, and it would be nice to already use it with godot-cpp in the 4.5 release (rather than in half a year from now).

If everyone was on board with this (including the production folks), I think it could be OK for 4.5. It mirrors what Godot has already had internally for a long time, so there isn't too much we could get wrong about this API.

Let's chat about it at next week's GDExtension team meeting

@Bromeon:

> The risk is more that an API is stabilized that then has to be indefinitely supported, whereas adding it after would give the whole 4.6 cycle for review, testing and feedback.

Yeah, I agree. I only starting digging into this problem on Monday, so this would be a very quick turn around for making a core change to the GDExtension interface. Especially given that the problem has existed since Godot 4.0 was released, so it isn't a 4.5 release blocker or anything

However, like I said above, as far as APIs go, this one does seem pretty safe given that mirrors a fairly stable internal API (even Godot 3.0 has the `p_pad_align` argument [on these functions](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/3.0/core/os/memory.h#L56))
author: dsnopek

body: Discussed at the GDExtension meeting, and we're OK with waiting for Godot 4.6 on this one. The issue has existed since Godot 4.0 and doesn't come up as often as other support issues. And this will allow us time to really test that debug and release builds can mixed and there are no other issues

body:
Thanks, this is a really great idea! I love using `scrcpy` :-)

I tested on my phone (Samsung  S21 Ultra) and it worked, but I have two notes:

- Having these settings on the export preset doesn't feel quite right. They aren't really settings for export, because if I do a plain export, it doesn't run `scrcpy` - it's only when you use the "Remote Deploy" button that it does something. Also, if I've got multiple Android presets (which I frequently do when targeting different standalone VR headsets), I don't really want to configure this differently for each one. So, maybe this would be better in Project Settings or even Editor Settings?
- If I disable only "Virtual Display", then `scrcpy` won't start, and gives this error to the terminal that I launched Godot from:
  ```
  scrcpy 3.3.1 <https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy>
  ERROR: --display-ime-policy is only supported on a secondary display
  ```
  I need to also disable "Local Ime" for it to start correctly. Two things about this:
    - Disabling "Virtual Display" should probably automatically disable "Local Ime"
    - The error from `scrcpy` should be exposed in the editor somewhere, not just printed to the terminal that launched Godot, or at least give some indication in the editor that there was an error running `scrcpy`

It is usable without integration, but this is at least 3 action: export, install, run vs. one click from the editor with integration.
author: bruvzg

body:
> I don’t see any real benefit to adding this to core. scrcpy already works fine without integration, and adding to core feels unnecessary to me. We should first evaluate whether it really needs to be part of the editor at all.

@syntaxerror247 I can see value in adding the feature to core; it is similar to [Android Studio device streaming feature](https://developer.android.com/studio/run/device#device-mirroring), and core integration would make it easier for users to discover and use.

I agree that some of the settings should be moved to the editor settings but I think whether or not it's enabled should be device specific. For example on mobile VR devices, the feature may be able to mirror the screen, but keyboard interactions are unlikely to work. 
So the feature should include a toggle in the remote deploy device selection drop-down to dictate whether or not the feature should be enabled.

Another TODO after we aligned on this PR is to update the Android documentation to describe the feature and provide instructions for setting it up.

> @syntaxerror247 I can see value in adding the feature to core; it is similar to [Android Studio device streaming feature](https://developer.android.com/studio/run/device#device-mirroring), and core integration would make it easier for users to discover and use.

I get the point that it makes things easier to use, but I feel that integrating this feature directly into the editor might make it seem like the recommended way to test apps, which I don't think is ideal. I sometimes use `scrcpy` to test with different dpi, but most of the time I prefer running the app directly on my device, as it gives me a better sense of how it actually feels.

I still think it's better to keep this as an optional tool rather than part of the editor.

body:
As I mentioned in https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12314#issuecomment-2830383998, given the Compatibility fallback is supposed to work automatically, I'm not sure we should add an option that is just here to work around a bug.

> But u know, sometimes downloaded a project with Fallback to OpenGL 3 disabled

Projects that disable the fallback likely have a good reason to do so. For example, visuals may be completely broken and look misleading if edited while using Compatibility. If they still want to be editable on unsupported devices, they should use [feature tags](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/tutorials/export/feature_tags.html) to only apply the project setting change to the `template` feature tag to leave editor usage unaffected. (`template` should be used here instead of `editor_hint`, as running the project from the editor is likely something you'll want to do.)

author: goatchurchprime

body:
@adamscott 

The deeper proposal is to separate the Audio Input (microphone code) from Audio Output (speakers) and move this code into a default microphone feed class.

Unfortunately the Godot codebase does not make this separation easy to do. 

Here is why.

The Godot `AudioDriver` class (which contains the [single microphone input buffer](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/0400b703e4c95e7de0ee98688267b98a5fb5e358/servers/audio/audio_server.h#L61) ) has a derived class for each platform: `AudioDriverPulseAudio`, `AudioDriverXAudio2`, `AudioDriverWeb`, `AudioDriverOpenSL`, `AudioDriverALSA`, `AudioDriverCoreAudio`, and `AudioDriverWASAPI`.

Each of these classes manages the single input and single output for that platform -- **sometimes with the same function**.

For example, in `AudioDriverWASAPI` there is a 300 line initialization function that has a parameter `p_input` to say whether it is setting up the input or the output device:

[Error AudioDriverWASAPI::audio_device_init(AudioDeviceWASAPI *p_device, bool p_input, bool p_reinit, bool p_no_audio_client_3)](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/drivers/wasapi/audio_driver_wasapi.cpp#L199)

Here are the options of what can be done.

### Option 1: Separate the Audio Input and Output at a deep level

This requires me to write a new `AudioInputDriver` class along with 7 derived classes `AudioInputDriverPulseAudio`, `AudioInputDriverXAudio2`, `AudioInputDriverWeb`, `AudioInputDriverOpenSL`, `AudioInputDriverALSA`, `AudioInputDriverCoreAudio`, and `AudioInputDriverWASAPI` and cut-and-paste the input related functions into each of these for each platform.

The case of `AudioInputDriverWASAPI` would require me to copy-paste that 300 line `audio_device_init()` function, which would cause complaints about code duplication.

### Option 2 (what I have implemented): Touch the AudioDriver code as little as humanly possible so nothing breaks:

Leave all the code and its technical debt (in relation to the feature of multiple microphones) in place, including its [single microphone input buffer](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/0400b703e4c95e7de0ee98688267b98a5fb5e358/servers/audio/audio_server.h#L61) on which the 7  different platform implementations depend, and simply access this single buffer from the one and only `MicrophoneFeed`.

### Option 3: Something in between

Leave the `AudioDriver` class and its 7 derived classes as they are but extend the core function  [AudioDriver::input_buffer_write(int32_t sample)](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/0400b703e4c95e7de0ee98688267b98a5fb5e358/servers/audio/audio_server.cpp#L102) to take a `microphone_feed_id` parameter that enables each platform to push the audio data to one of multiple microphone buffers in the core class.

Since none of the platforms implement multiple microphones, the value of `microphone_feed_id` will always be zero.  So although I could write code that looked like it did something in terms managing and looking up multiple `MicrophoneFeed` objects, it would do nothing more than the current PR, so I think what is in the PR is more honest because it is not trying to fool anyone with code that doesn't do anything.

In my opinion the appropriate time to implement the code that can manage multiple MicrophoneFeeds is when at least one of the platforms' AudioDrivers has been extended to support it, because otherwise its implementation will be speculative and likely to be wrong.
author: goatchurchprime

body:
I made a similar implementation of a `MicrophoneServer`/`MicrophoneFeed` ([branch here](https://github.com/BuzzLord/godot/tree/microphone-server)) but with a slightly different interface. I wasn't happy with some aspects of it (I forget specifically what, though...), and so didn't turn it into a PR after seeing this PR, but maybe we can pull something from it into here.

My implementation has the `MicrophoneServer` create a `MicrophoneFeed` on demand, based on an `input_device` name (which comes from the `AudioServer.get_input_device_list`). Multiple feeds can be used to record mic data independently, since each feed has its own buffer offset into the AudioDriver `input_buffer`.

However, since only one real microphone exists in the `AudioServer` (as currently implemented), the feed will throw an error if you try to start recording with a different device than the active one. I removed access to setting the `input_device` via `AudioServer`, and only let it happen through the MicrophoneServer/Feed.
author: BuzzLord

body:
I talked to @goatchurchprime at GodotFest and found out about this PR that way 🙂 I implemented a voip GDExtension for our next game, too (still very much WIP, but can be found here: https://github.com/mrTag/listenclosely ) and I'm sharing the pain of the currently very cumbersome and delay inducing way of accessing the microphone stream in a GDExtension. 

With the current wave of indie "Friend Slop" games (Peak, R.E.P.O. and the like) it is very important for Godot to have a good voip solution ready and for that we need a good way to access the microphone stream directly. I think the `MicrophoneFeed` solution with "Option 2" is a good one, since it doesn't change the core all that much and so it can be ready quickly, while still being open to extensive changes in the background down the line. 

All this to say: Please don't forget about this PR, as the issue is more important than you might think. Thank you!

Although this PR is related to a number of existing issues that are linked in the main description, this PR does not fix or close a number of these issues; instead, it simply provides a workaround. The primary purpose of this PR is to close the proposal for direct access to microphone input for VoIP. The other issues will still remain open.

(Said differently, this PR description should not use the "fixes `#...`" for issues that it does not fully resolve; it's better to just mention them as "related to". It's good to have a list of related issues and also good to distinguish which issues are actually closed by this PR.)

author: allenwp

body:
I don't think it's the style of the XML tag that's the issue, but that the documentation is referring to the property override, not to the original definition in the base class.

The difference between a paired tag and a self-closing tag is that property overrides don't have a separate description, so there is no need a space for the description text. I don't think this should be changed, but if it were, you'd need to change `DocTools`.

Instead, I suggest you check if the reference to the property override is correct. If not, it should be replaced with a reference to the original definition in the base class. Otherwise, we should add support for this to `make_rst.py`.
author: dalexeev

body:
Hi, this is an interesting proposition that we've been discussing a few times!

I have also made an attempt at implementing it, which you may want to have a look at: https://github.com/Ivorforce/godot/tree/constexpr-string

I don't exactly remember the outcome, but I believe the main problem I encountered was that users needed to use a custom macro (`SString`) to make use of `constexpr` strings. Ideally, the `constexpr` constructor would be automatically chosen for `char[]` values, although I don't know if that's possible (mind you, I haven't looked at your implementation yet!).
author: Ivorforce

body:
> I don't exactly remember the outcome, but I believe the main problem I encountered was that users needed to use a custom macro (`SString`) to make use of `constexpr` strings. Ideally, the `constexpr` constructor would be automatically chosen for `char[]` values, although I don't know if that's possible (mind you, I haven't looked at your implementation yet!).

Yes, I encountered the same issue. I believe the key here is that we must somehow write `static constexpr str_storage = "literals";`, because currently we cannot dynamically allocate memory at compile time, so the user must provide external memory to store the string. I provide a template struct `ComptimeString` which is basicly identical to your macro. Haven't find a way to automatically convert string literals to `constexpr String` yet as it is not possible now to get the space for `refcount` and `size` in functions.

body:
The crash is not reproducible on API level >= 28. It seems Android handles clipboard access differently on newer versions.

Since this solution blocks the thread, do we want to restrict it only to API <= 27 and continue calling the method directly on newer versions?
author: syntaxerror247

body:
> Let's use a [`FutureTask`](https://developer.android.com/reference/java/util/concurrent/FutureTask) instead. For api <= 27, you can submit the `FutureTask` to the `mainExecutor`. For api > 27, you can just call `FutureTask#run()` directly.

Quick clarification; you should create persistent `Callable` fields to wrap the logic for each method:
- on api > 27, you call the `Callable` directly and return their result (this way we're not allocated new objects for each method call)
- on api <= 27, you wrap the `Callable` in a `FutureTask` and submit it to the `mainExecutor`. We're allocating new `FutureTask` objects on each invocation, but there's no other options.

author: twrightsman

body:
The logic should be:
- If it's built-in ICU with static data or system ICU, only call `u_init` and set loaded flag (we assume data is OK, or system ICU will take care of it).
- If it's built-in ICU without static data, try loading data from `res:\\` and then call `u_init` regardless if data was loaded/found, but loaded flag is set only if data is loaded (to disable features using it).

body:
Thank you for the Review!

> However, if you orbit while holding Alt, it will only focus on the point when you release the mouse button. The orbiting operation will be relative to the old position, then it'll snap to the new position after you release the mouse.
>
> I think it should set the new point of origin for orbiting immediately when you click. It would likely match user expectations better as I've understood from the proposal.

That was intentional because that is also the default behaviour in Blender and Unity. This is because both differentiate between mouse clicking and dragging, which makes it necessary to fire the click on mouse up after checking for a drag on mouse down.

In Blender, holding `MMB down` is orbit, holding `Alt`+`MMB down` is switch orthographic views and clicking `Alt`+`MMB up` is focus pointer.

In Unity, holding `MMB down` is panning, holding `Alt`+`MMB down` is orbit and clicking `MMB up` is focus pointer.

I use both of them regularly and this is the behaviour I would have expected. Of course that doesn't mean we should blindly follow what they have done. I did a quick test with focus on `Alt`+`MMB down` and it did feel a bit weird at first, but was quite pleasant to use after a while.

Though I now also realize that hardcoding `Alt`+`MMB` is probably not a good idea because Godot supports [Navigation Schemes](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/3d/introduction_to_3d.html#navigating-the-3d-environment) and the `Tablet/Trackpad` scheme does use `Alt`+`MMB` for orbit. So it might still be necessary to make that distinction between mouse down and up. Will look into that.
author: priahoud

body:
> Though I now also realize that hardcoding `Alt`+`MMB` is probably not a good idea because Godot supports [Navigation Schemes](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/3d/introduction_to_3d.html#navigating-the-3d-environment) and the `Tablet/Trackpad` scheme does use `Alt`+`MMB` for orbit. So it might still be necessary to make that distinction between mouse down and up. Will look into that.

Without the ability to specify mouse input for command shortcuts like blender does, the best way forward was to just specify another field in the navigation schemas. This way, people can easily choose their preferred setup. Hope that it ok.

The action now also triggers immediately on mouse down.

Let me know what you think!
author: priahoud

> Tested again, I think it works pretty well now. I've tried all navigation schemes and was wondering why **Tablet/Trackpad** used Ctrl + Alt + Middle mouse button instead of just Alt + Middle mouse button though.
>
> Tablet/Trackpad is a special case when it comes to navigation, as it will work without holding down any mouse buttons. Just hold down Alt and move the mouse to orbit (Ctrl for zoom, Shift for pan).

That would be me being confused about how that scheme actually works.

I don't have any experience with Godot on Trackpad/Tablet devices and wasn't aware of what the "Emulate 3 Button Mouse" setting does. I thought `Alt` + `MMB` to focus would conflict with the orbit setting, but since - as you said yourself -  "Emulate 3 Button Mouse" actually means "no mouse button necessary for orbit, pan and zoom", it should be safe to use `Alt` + `MMB` to focus on Trackpads and Tablets.

Thanks again for the feedback!
Commit incoming

author: priahoud

body:
Ok, with #109029 merged we are back in business, but there is still an issue with the way navigation schemes are evaluated. They basically don't differentiate between all possible combinations of modifier keys, leading to situations where focusing to the pointer also interferes with another navigation function.

For example in the Godot scheme, orbit navigation is configured to be `MMB` without modifier keys. `Alt`+`MMB` would be a perfect shortcut for focus pointer (as that is also the shortcut in blender), but the system doesn't take non-assigned modifier keys into account. As far as i can see, it just prioritizes the shortcut with the most defined modifiers. As a result, both functions would be activated at the same time when pressing `Alt`+`MMB`.  The same is true for the Maya scheme and panning.

Generally, a choice of a shortcut combo for focus pointer in the ui does not make much sense when half of them interfere with other ones. I am currently checking to see if that limitation can be worked around but until then, I have removed the navigation scheme parts for now.
author: priahoud

body: Mouse buttons are not handled using shortcuts, this is probably hard-coded in the editor somewhere. You can modify the code to properly take modifiers into account.
author: KoBeWi

body:
I implemented this not knowing about this PR. For initial testing I just used hardcoded ALT+MMB release in `_sinput()` and had the whole thing togglable via a flag in editor settings.

This is too useful to be thrown under the bus on the account of navigational schemes. Even putting it on all 3 modifier keys + a mouse button would be preferable to not having it. That way it can circumvent any navigational scheme. 

Currently testing it precisely like that. I intercept the motion event at the start of `_sinput()` and simply exit if ALT+CTRL+SHIFT are all down. For the button event, I check all 3 modifiers, if they are down I execute the focusing code and exit. In this case the actual button could be configured but all 3 modifiers stay set in stone. This is still better than changing it around per nav scheme to avoid interference. 3 mods are kinda finger bending but it's fine since this is a trigger command and no holding is required. The functionality is worth putting the 3 fingers down occasionally :)

I also have it on a single hotkey analogous to "focus selection (F)". That works fine as well and is probably the least "abrasive" way to include it.

Another option is sticking it as an item at the end of selection popup that appears on ALT+RMB (in default scheme)

author: DexterFstone

body: It needs a proposal.
author: KoBeWi

body:
This shouldn't be exposed as is, it currently is not a good fit for type hints.
As an example, when a user changes a line to `var x := myfunc()`, then you may need to run your language parser on the whole file or all files for you to get the information needed to determine the type. And even if you try to cache the type info, changing multiple lines at once could still invalidate it. But the parser callback runs every single time a line changes and it has to return the data in that call, which leads to big performance issues.
Also, if you change the return type of your function then there is no way to change the inlay hint at the variable declaration line besides manually editing the line to re-trigger parsing. This is the biggest issue, there needs to be a way to get and set the inline objects from anywhere.

Also the current approach can't handle multiple lines, even though GDScript would allow it to work with the inline color pickers.
We should get GDScript inlay type hints working in the Script Editor first, to get a better API for this that can then be exposed. Eg. how it works together with the Color object system, when it should update, if it needs more info. After exposing it, it will be much harder to change.

Other concerns:
- The click handler should be separate and optional, since you may not want to do anything on click. Or maybe it should be removed so the regular input handling can be used.
- It is currently required that the parser returns an array of dictionaries and that it has certain keys, we probably want to allow more flexibility.
- We may want to have TextEdit store the inline data itself and only put a single value into the text rather than entire dictionaries. I'm not sure if allowing any large inline objects directly into the TextParagraph would cause issues. And it should be easier to provide access to the inline objects from anywhere this way. 
- When parsing, the user may need the line number, to be able to check adjacent lines or results they may have from their own parser. Though we should make sure that the line number isn't saved in the object, since it won't be updated.
- There needs to be a way to remove the inline object without re-parsing too.
- We may want to remove the parsing callback entirely, the user has signals when the text is edited which can be used instead.
- Does the inline object work correctly when at the end of the line? The inline color only works before a character, so parts of the logic may depend on that.
- Do we want to keep the `width_ratio` approach for determining the width of it? Currently it isn't even really used with the Color picker because they are square, and needing to divide by the font height is awkward. Though it might be needed for zooming in.
- Do we want to expose this on TextEdit, even though all of our use cases are on CodeEdit?
author: kitbdev

body:
I wasn't literally suggesting that this functionality should be used for inlay hints, I was just using them to demonstrate the functionality through this API.

> The click handler should be separate and optional, since you may not want to do anything on click. Or maybe it should be removed so the regular input handling can be used.

You can set it to `Callable()` and it gets disabled. Maybe that aspect should get documented?

> It is currently required that the parser returns an array of dictionaries and that it has certain keys, we probably want to allow more flexibility.

What more flexibility do you want? Until the advent of structs in GDScript this isn't really possible.

> We may want to have TextEdit store the inline data itself and only put a single value into the text rather than entire dictionaries. I'm not sure if allowing any large inline objects directly into the TextParagraph would cause issues. And it should be easier to provide access to the inline objects from anywhere this way.

Word wrapping works as expected. I'd say providing inline objects through the parser callback makes sense. If users need to access inline objects from anywhere, they should do that separately.

> When parsing, the user may need the line number, to be able to check adjacent lines or results they may have from their own parser. Though we should make sure that the line number isn't saved in the object, since it won't be updated.

I don't think this is a problem. You can always calculate the line from the index, and most things won't need it.

> There needs to be a way to remove the inline object without re-parsing too.

I don't think this is true?

> We may want to remove the parsing callback entirely, the user has signals when the text is edited which can be used instead.

I like how the parser callback is called every time the text changes automatically, because it solves a bunch of bugs with inline objects desyncing with the text.

> Does the inline object work correctly when at the end of the line? The inline color only works before a character, so parts of the logic may depend on that.

Actually it doesn't, so further work should be done to fix that. I'd say it's not really in the scope of this PR.

> Do we want to keep the `width_ratio` approach for determining the width of it? Currently it isn't even really used with the Color picker because they are square, and needing to divide by the font height is awkward. Though it might be needed for zooming in.

I think we might as well, since that's how it already works.

> Do we want to expose this on TextEdit, even though all of our use cases are on CodeEdit?

I don't see why not. I think inline objects aren't necessarily a "code editing" feature, since you can use them for other stuff.
author: sockeye-d
sockeye-d

body:
> I wasn't literally suggesting that this functionality should be used for inlay hints, I was just using them to demonstrate the functionality through this API.

If this API shouldn't be used for inlay hints, then that should not be used as an example in the documentation.
Also what is the use case then?

Inlay hints is one of the most obvious uses for this functionality, but if users try it they will quickly run into limitations. So it is better to be able fully support it.

> You can set it to Callable() and it gets disabled. Maybe that aspect should get documented?

I thought this wouldn't change the cursor correctly, but it looks like it does, so that part is fine.
But this only allows for left click interactions, no hover or right click and it doesn't work with the normal gui input handling override.

>What more flexibility do you want? Until the advent of structs in GDScript this isn't really possible.

I don't see how structs would help the flexibility. I meant remove requirements to make it easier to use, and prevent issues, since it will just do nothing silently when the user doesn't return an array of dictionaries with the exact needed keys.
Keys like "width_ratio" can be optional, returning a single dictionary can be allowed, and out of order columns would work (currently it doesn't).

> Word wrapping works as expected. 

I meant performance issues if the user adds lots of data to the dictionaries. 
Also word wrap doesn't work correctly if it is too long.

> I'd say providing inline objects through the parser callback makes sense. If users need to access inline objects from anywhere, they should do that separately.

It's not possible to access the inline objects from anywhere, which is part of the issue.

> I don't think this is a problem. You can always calculate the line from the index, and most things won't need it.

What do you mean by index? The only information currently provided to the parser is the line text. 
And the only things that won't need it are what can be parsed entirely from that one line, which is limited.

> > There needs to be a way to remove the inline object without re-parsing too.

> I don't think this is true?

The only way to do it right now is to change the inline object parser to invalidate and reparse all lines to remove them. This doesn't give much control if you want to manage them.

> Actually it doesn't, so further work should be done to fix that. I'd say it's not really in the scope of this PR.

Fixing this issue may require changing the API, so it should at least be considered before exposing it.

> I had also thought about integrating the inlay hints as a an optional field in the return dictionaries of SyntaxHighlighter

I don't think putting it into SyntaxHighlighter would make sense, it is unrelated to syntax highlighting and we should keep the logic separate.

author: Repiteo

body: Why not serialize `NaN` and `Infinity` as `"NaN"` and `"Infinity"` (or `"-Infinity"`)? This is what C# does on an opt-in basis ([link](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.text.json.serialization.jsonnumberhandling)).
author: Joy-less

body:
Two problems with the string representation:

* If a field is specified as a number in a JSON schema, `"Infinity"` is not of that type, so such output would not be valid for that schema, while `1e99999` is still compliant.
* If reading in data without any schema or other type known in advance, it will be interpreted as a string instead of a number, which means that the exporter would change the data type, while `1e99999` is always imported as a number.

For NaN, `null` also isn't a number, so either way breaks both of these cases, but it at least implies that the data is "invalid" while still being readable as JSON, unlike `"NaN"` which looks like a valid string. EDIT: Actually, `null` could be collapsed to `0.0` by some importers, so perhaps `"NaN"` is better and clearer.

Anyway, if we want this functionality, it must be opt-in (as already proposed above, so, good). However, this would mean we need to add a parameter to `stringify`, which would change the API, and we'd need to register a compatibility method. This could be done as a separate PR, or if it's really strongly wanted I guess I could put it in this PR. But I'm thinking that this would need a proposal, while this PR by itself is good as-is.

author: clayjohn

body:
Is this not a breaking change to existing behaviour?

Currently, in Godot 4.5, if you run:
```gdscript
print(JSON.stringify(0))
print(JSON.stringify(INF))
print(JSON.stringify(-INF))
print(JSON.stringify(NAN))
```

You get:
```gdscript
0
inf
-inf
nan
```

Relevant code is in `String::num`: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/16a11ac88b3aedac2825ec570910f34fb40f3f98/core/string/ustring.cpp#L1434
author: Joy-less

body:
> It feels like `ERR_FAIL_COND` should be robust to the engine being constructed / torn down. It should certainly not involve any `StringName` instances, right?

Error logging can do anything, since you can register custom loggers from extensions/scripts/modules/whatever.

However, the fact that `CoreBind::OS::LoggerBind` (which hosts the script-based loggers) is still alive and registered with `OS::_logger` at the point in the program shown in #108838 is somewhat questionable, so that could be another angle we could take, but I figured it would just be a matter of time until someone does add some other usage of `StringName` in some part of the `_err_print_error`[^1] stack anyway, whether directly or indirectly, so we might as well squash the bug at the `StringName` level instead.

I will admit though that this mindset does open a Pandora's box of "should all core types stop using `ERR_*` on the off-chance that they might be used as part of error logging and cause a stack overflow?" which is somewhat problematic.

[^1]: There might very well be already, just that nobody has triggered it in this particular context.
author: mihe

body: Maybe a simple `static thread_local` re-entry guard in `_err_print_error` could be another approach as well, where we fall back to `fprintf(stderr, ...)` if we find ourselves triggering an error from within an error?
author: mihe

body:
> However, the fact that CoreBind::OS::LoggerBind (which hosts the script-based loggers) is still alive and registered with OS::_logger at the point in the program shown in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/108838 is somewhat questionable, so that could be another angle we could take, but I figured it would just be a matter of time until someone does add some other usage of StringName in some part of the _err_print_error[1](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108839#user-content-fn-1-9f1a9618b9d828c2309a25071172c912) stack anyway, whether directly or indirectly, so we might as well squash the bug at the StringName level instead.

That's a very good point.
I think I would expect loggers to not work with any types that are capable of logging errors. So that means either using direct logging for core types, or discouraging their use in loggers (and not doing it at all from our end). But not using the loggers for `String` seems unrealistic, so perhaps we do just have to avoid using them in at least our own loggers.
author: Ivorforce

body:
> So that means either using direct logging for core types, or discouraging their use in loggers (and not doing it at all from our end).

Since you can have custom loggers defined from things like scripts, a good chunk of the GDScript VM (and whatever other third-party script VM) is now potentially involved in this. With that kind of scope I would argue trying to prevent/discourage core types from being used in loggers is even more unrealistic than not using loggers in core types.

I feel like a simple reentrancy guard in `_err_print_error` (and I guess a separate one for `__print_line` and `__print_line_rich` as well) might be the better holistic approach. That way we should be able to squash any infinite recursion from errors/printing, regardless of origin, albeit with the quirk that you'll likely see the message printed to `stderr`/`stdout` first and then any loggers.

With that we could probably even revert #85397 and #89490 if we wanted to I guess.

I put up another PR for consideration: #108860

body:
> I didn't get an infinite recursion on master ([71a9948](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/71a9948157dc2d5cc71fcb456c9d96656678757d))

On `master` you'd be covered by the reentrancy guard that this PR removes from `CoreBind::OS::LoggerBind`, which just does an early-out for the script-based loggers, so you won't get any infinite recursion there when dealing with script-based loggers specifically.

The MRP was mostly just an easy way to test these new fallbacks, now that the reentrancy guard in `CoreBind::OS::LoggerBind` is gone.

> One thing I'm noting is that there is no "following error" in the last example. It's particularly unclear to me which is the "following error" and which is the "another error".

Done! That does indeed read better.

author: kaganguru

body:
Bringing in some comments from a [related proposal](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/6151) to consolidate. :)

I think the majority of use cases for lines in games will be non-stationary. And in the cases where there is "curving", the curvedness comes usually not from a Bézier, but rather from gravity or otherwise derived from the motion of an object. In these cases, modifying a Bézier would introduce unnecessary overhead, especially since most of these cases, you'd be changing every point, every frame.

Furthermore, the use of a nested curve resource makes less sense for dynamically updating lines because in those cases, you would not need to reuse the curve resource - it would be transient state that is unique to the node that is rendering it.

An alternative would be to dispense of the need for a nested `Curve3D` resource at all, and make a more agnostic `LineMesh` resource that simply stores line data locally. It would be completely agnostic in terms of how it "derives" this data, and that way it remains performant, light and flexible. If a user wants to use a `Curve3D` to drive the shape, they can do that easily on their own by setting the points, which a small price to pay for what I believe is ultimately a more niche use case for lines. In the same way, they can use a sine wave, or audio bus data, etc. to drive the points.

An agnostic `LineMesh` would also create less user expectation for a type of authoring UX. e.g., for a Bézier, you could use a `Path3D` for authoring and pipe its curve data into the `LineMesh`. For a laser pointer, you could simply edit the arrays on the mesh. For dangling rope, you would have a tool script that generates the points from authored endpoints, etc.

To aid with that, I think it would make sense to have utility functions. A good example of this is [Mesh.get_faces()](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_mesh.html#class-mesh-method-get-faces). It effectively removes the need for a tight coupling between `Mesh` and `ConcavePolygonShape3D` by providing a way for the former to fetch data that is consumable by the latter. In our case here, we could for example have a `LineMesh.copy_curve()` function or something similar, which could even expose parameters to control whether you copy the roll, the sampling frequency, etc. etc.

And finally, this would create a smaller PR that would be easier to test/approve/merge. It opens a lot of doors, and more specific subclasses could always be proposed later if there is a need.

**In summary:**
WIthin the scope of visual lines in games, Béziers are not a common enough use case to justify making the most abstract form of a line mesh being a `CurveMesh` driven by a `Curve3D`. It creates overhead for the much more common use cases that require efficient hotpath execution. Recommend creating a more abstract `LineMesh` instead.
author: MajorMcDoom

body:
> An alternative would be to dispense of the need for a nested Curve3D resource at all, and make a more agnostic LineMesh resource that simply stores line data locally. It would be completely agnostic in terms of how it "derives" this data, and that way it remains performant, light and flexible. If a user wants to use a Curve3D to drive the shape, they can do that easily on their own by setting the points, which a small price to pay for what I believe is ultimately a more niche use case for lines. In the same way, they can use a sine wave, or audio bus data, etc. to drive the points.

I disagree. A single curve 3d resource can help author many shapes at once. Imagine you're building grass, or procedurally generating a bridge. In that case deriving the shape and bake data from a single curve 3d resource will be required. 

And you're missing the fact that you would need a whole another path editing plugin that needs maintaining. A unified way to edit the line is most definitely the right way to go with it, and curve 3d resource is there to aid.

In the other hand I don't see a notable disadvantage of using a curve 3d resource as the data storage.
author: kaganguru

body:
> I disagree. A single curve 3d resource can help author many shapes at once. Imagine you're building grass, or procedurally generating a bridge. In that case deriving the shape and bake data from a single curve 3d resource will be required.

Some use cases being aided by Curve3D does not mean Curve3D is a good base representation. Nothing is preventing the user from generating grass or bridges from a Curve3D. That doesn't mean a lightning strike or a 5-pointed star should be. Also a bridge isn't any kind of line, so is beyond the scope of discussion.

> And you're missing the fact that you would need a whole another path editing plugin that needs maintaining. A unified way to edit the line is most definitely the right way to go with it, and curve 3d resource is there to aid.

As previously mentioned, Path3D already exists to edit paths. There is no such thing as a "unified" way to edit lines. Lines are an extremely broad category of use cases, most of which are procedural and require no editing. Not all lines are paths, and not all paths are lines. But when a line *is* a path, Path3D can be used to edit the source data, which is then used to build the line. Just as you would use it to build a bridge, or to make flowing traffic, or anything else. Lines aren't special in that sense.

body: The special case with metadata is that you retrieve the data using the name of the metadata. And since the name is a string you dont get any autocompletion or validity check while writing the code, which can lead to unexpected errors if you wrote it with incorrect capitalisation. This makes it more important to be able to see the exact name of the metadata. Additionally metadata is a string that you define in the editor, so an argument can be made that it should follow the same rules as other types of strings you can define in the editor, like groups and resource names (both of which dont have any special styling)

Engine.time_scale doesn't effect audio at all I think. Which is a good thing as games can add brief pauses for impact without having to set process mode to always on playback nodes. Because 'get_tree().paused = true' does stop playback. 

The original game I found the bug in uses 3d navigation for pathfinding. I use  `get_next_path_position()`  on each agent and then apply velocity to the agent's body. Which the navigation function is not delta dependent to my knowledge. So ultimately the bug came back to move_and_slide there anyways 

I think this code probably fixes the majority of bugs caused by setting time_scale to 0 most people will encounter. But probably is a good idea to keep an eye out for other functions that should be skipped when time_scale is 0. Or atleast throw a warning of unpredictable behavior

> `JoltPhysicsServer3D::step` does more than just step the simulation, and since you're not doing an early return in `PhysicsServer3D::flush_queries` (which you arguably shouldn't) you may or may not end up with some strange issues now that one will not always be followed by the other.

Thinking about this some more, you would almost certainly be changing the behavior of `_integrate_forces` with this change, as it gets called as part of `PhysicsServer3D::flush_queries` for all active bodies, but that list is wiped and re-populated on every `PhysicsServer3D::step`, so you would most likely no longer be calling `_integrate_forces` while time scale is 0.

This would apply to Godot Physics as well.

body:
Changes look good to me, but I'm a bit suspect about the SM 6.1 change.

As per [this page](https://github.com/microsoft/DirectXShaderCompiler/wiki/Shader-Model-6.1), the additions to SM 6.1 seem to be:
```md
[SV_ViewID](https://github.com/microsoft/DirectXShaderCompiler/wiki/SV_ViewID). This feature enables instancing of the graphics pipeline by "view", in a manner that is orthogonal to draw instancing.
[SV_Barycentrics](https://github.com/microsoft/DirectXShaderCompiler/wiki/SV_Barycentrics). This is a new system-generated value available in pixel shaders, used for example to perform interpolation over small or unaligned values like a few bits from a 32-bit value.
```
ViewID is only relevant for multiview (XR), so unless it is enabled, it won't get compiled. And I don't think there's a GLSL construct for barycentrics. I'm not sure the provided comment is good enough of a justification to bump the minimum SM required unless we get something that proves it necessary.

body:
> LGTM, only small question is if it's safe to hardcode `true` in a few calls to `_rt_attach_textures`, instead of checking `render_target_get_depth_has_stencil`.

Bastiaan and I chatted about this on RocketChat, so a note for folks only following GitHub:

The hard-coded `true` is in the case where we've allocated an intermediate depth buffer for MSAA (and we always allocate those with a format that has a stencil component); we only need to check `render_target_get_depth_has_stencil()` for the case where we are rendering directly to the render target, which may have a depth buffer allocated by WebXR

body: In my testing, the `begin` method sometimes received the window size from the project settings, sometimes the window size after resizing for embed game mode. If it was reliably the window size from the project settings, we could use it to force a video output resolution.
author: berarma

body:
> In my testing, the `begin` method sometimes received the window size from the project settings, sometimes the window size after resizing for embed game mode. If it was reliably the window size from the project settings, we could use it to force a video output resolution.

It probably depends on whether you were done resizing the window by the time the first time was drawn. I guess we should always make it use the viewport size project setting instead, so that it's  more reliable.

**Edit:** I pushed a second commit which does exactly that.
author: Calinou

body:
This would only work for stretch mode `Viewport`, wouldn't it? It also ignores the size override settings, is that alright?

**Edit**: It also doesn't tackle the uncertainty of the resolution passed to the `begin` method. I thought you were going to use directly the project settings and ignore `p_movie_size`.
author: berarma

body:
> This would only work for stretch mode Viewport, wouldn't it? It also ignores the size override settings, is that alright?

I've tested the commit with the `disabled` stretch mode and it still works. However, this means the size override does not affect the recording resolution anymore, but this was existing behavior when using the `viewport` stretch mode already.

I guess we should use the `size_override` project setting when the stretch mode is `disabled` or `canvas_items`, and `size` when the stretch mode is `viewport` to fully preserve existing behavior.

**Edit:** I've pushed a third commit that does the above. The output video size is also now printed on startup as well.

body: This appears to be a bug with `intersect_shape()`. CC @godotengine/physics
author: YeldhamDev

body:
It's not a bug. You will end up with multiple hits for the same body in some cases, even for regular rigid bodies, like if it has multiple shapes.

The fix in this PR is correct as far as the `intersect_shape` call goes, but if this code is just looking to get the closest/deepest hit against any body instead then [`get_rest_info`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/classes/class_physicsdirectspacestate3d.html#class-physicsdirectspacestate3d-method-get-rest-info) is probably what you should be using instead.

Urgh... Looks like `MSVC` is incorrectly using the implicit `Variant` -> `Vector<Variant>` conversion to pass into `Array`, which makes the tests fail.
It's disconcerting to think it might be making such wrong calls in other parts of our code as well. I'm not sure how to fix this yet, open to suggestions. body:
I would appreciate any thoughts or comments on this, it's my first PR (and second contribution) for Godot.
 
Something else to consider here is if I could add some more generic way to make the texture preview applicable, since would like to move my procedural texture class from using Texture2DRD to being based on Texture2D and handling the other parts myself.
Reason being that sometimes I would need to be able to switch from using a rd texture and other times a texture 2d as a the rendering server side, as decals for example don't support rd textures. 
My current solution is to have two separate classes, with separate bases, but that is messy since I cannot switch a texture from one to the other easily.
I was thinking perhaps to use utilise a some general logic like if the class is Texture2D, and it has functions for `_get_mipmap_count` and `_get_format` (inline with the existing override functions for Texture2D class) to provide the additional data that the texture preview is after then it will apply a preview.

Right now the texture preview class contains a lot of logic that is repeated for various texture subclasses, and it would be better OOP if that logic lived on the subclasses (perhaps even on an override function), each class could even mark itself with a const if it wanted texture preview to apply. This would both DRY up the TexturePreview class and move the logic to the responsible subclasses. Thoughts?

If there is an appetite for a feature that adds a generic procedural texture class to the core engine I can look at converting my GDscript work into c++ after this,  but that would be good chunk of work since it uses a custom set of compute pipeline resources along side some.

@LiveTrower I feel that the radio button selectors are both easier to use and nicer looking, but I'm happy to be outvoted and adjust it 😄 

As for mipmap visuals on other texture types, I have here only added a mipmap option to textures in the TexturePreview that support retrival of the number of mipmaps - I think it would perhaps make sense to expand the Texture class (or subclasses) with an implementation of the `get_format` function (with assisiated override `_get_format`) and a `get_mipmap_count` (and assositated _ override) as suggested in the feature proposal here: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12873
That way we could enable the texture preview more widely and rely on those functions without adding custom logic per class.
However, that expansion seems out of scope for this change to add the initial functionality.
Perhaps if you support it or have thoughts you could add them to that discussion?

On the matter of linear vs nearest - I am happy with nearest for texture previews (more clearly shows the "data" in the image for my purposes) but I'd happily support the addition of a selector for filtering 👍

author: DDarby-Lewis

body:
> So it failed again, I'm not really sure what it needs at this point though so any advice would be appreciated.

CI is failing because `get_format()` is no longer exposed in the ImageTexture and PortableCompressedTexture2D classes, even though it's now exposed in Texture2D instead (both ImageTexture and PortableCompressedTexture2D inherit from Texture2D). In terms of compatibility, this is acceptable for scripts, but GDExtensions will break. As I understand it, to preserve compatibility with existing projects, you'd need to expose dedicated `get_format()` method overrides in ImageTexture and PortableCompressedTexture2D. See [Handling compatibility breakages](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/contributing/development/handling_compatibility_breakages.html) in the documentation for details.

cc @dsnopek
author: Calinou

body:
I like that this allows us to implement the proposed KHR physics extensions for glTF. There was positive feedback in the animation meeting about the new shape.

Originally, I was hoping we could add functionality to the existing capsule shape to extend radius to be the mid-radius, and a taper property. The internal Jolt type is still SHAPE_CAPSULE. The problem @fire explained is we need to use mid_height instead of height, since the meaning of height as the outer height depends on the combined upper and lower radii. This explains why creating a new tapered collision shape is perhaps the most prudent choice, but I'd be interested to see if others have ideas on keeping one capsule type while still maintaining compatibility.

Most importantly, we need someone deeply involved in physics to also give this a look over.
